primary_diagnosis.name: Temporomandibular disorder — acute masticatory (masseter/temporalis) muscle spasm with trismus, on a background of fibromyalgia / central sensitisation

pathophysiology_summary: Sustained parafunctional bruxism (nocturnal grinding/clenching), amplified by stress, has driven the bilateral masseter and temporalis muscles into a sustained myofascial spasm, producing severe pain and marked trismus (restricted mouth opening). A background of fibromyalgia with central sensitisation lowers the pain threshold and broadens the pain (temples, neck, widespread body ache), so the presentation is dramatic and distressing out of proportion to any structural or infective pathology. The alarming picture (locked jaw, headache, systemic distress) prompts consideration of three dangerous mimics — giant cell arteritis (jaw claudication, visual loss, scalp tenderness), odontogenic deep space infection / Ludwig's angina (facial-neck swelling, fever, airway threat), and tetanus (trismus, generalised rigidity, risus sardonicus) — but each is screened negative here: temporal arteries are non-tender and pulsatile with no visual symptoms, there is no facial swelling/erythema/fluctuance and the patient is afebrile, and tetanus immunisation is current with a clear chronic-bruxism history and no generalised tetany. The correct disposition is conservative management of a benign muscular problem plus prompt in-person review for a short-course prescription muscle relaxant and to safeguard hydration, with explicit safety-netting for the mimics. The genuine acute risk is functional dehydration from pain-limited oral intake, not a time-critical emergency.

differential.diagnosis: TMD masticatory muscle spasm with trismus
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral cramping jaw-muscle pain with trismus radiating to temples in a distressed patient — classic masticatory myofascial spasm, especially with a bruxism/fibromyalgia background.
Trismus and severe facial pain can herald a spreading dental/deep-space infection (Ludwig's angina) with airway threat — must be actively excluded before reassurance.
Temple pain plus jaw symptoms in an adult raises giant cell arteritis — a sight-threatening emergency that must be screened for (visual symptoms, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication).
Trismus (lockjaw) is a cardinal early sign of tetanus — low probability but must be screened via immunisation status, wounds and generalised rigidity.
Widespread body ache and disproportionate distress point to a central sensitisation background amplifying the local jaw problem.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the alarming framing must not stampede the AI Doctor either into premature reassurance or into blanket emergency escalation — the three dangerous mimics should be listed important_not_to_miss and screened, with muscular spasm leading.

differential.diagnosis: TMD masticatory muscle spasm with trismus
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic bruxism, known fibromyalgia, no fever, pain constant (not chew-specific), simple analgesia ineffective — a muscular spasm picture consolidates.
No fever and no reported facial/neck swelling lower the probability, but chat cannot inspect the mouth — retained as a screen-and-safety-net item.
No visual disturbance, no scalp tenderness and pain not chew-specific argue against it, but it is retained for safety-netting.
Up-to-date immunisation, no wound, chronic bruxism history and no generalised rigidity effectively exclude tetanus.
Confirmed fibromyalgia flare contextualises the amplified pain and distress.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After history the muscular diagnosis should be clearly leading; tetanus can be excluded; the vascular and infective mimics are downgraded but should still be safety-netted because remote assessment cannot fully exclude them.

differential.diagnosis: TMD masticatory muscle spasm with trismus
differential.evidence_basis: Full negative dangerous-mimic screen (no visual/scalp symptoms, no neck swelling/airway symptoms, afebrile, vaccinated) with a positive muscular/bruxism/fibromyalgia picture.
No visual symptoms, no scalp tenderness, pain not jaw-claudication in pattern — clinically excluded on available information, retained only as a safety-net trigger.
Afebrile, no swelling under chin/neck, no drooling or breathing/swallowing difficulty — excluded on screen, retained as a safety-net trigger.
Contributory background amplifying the acute muscular pain.

differential_progression.scoring_note: With the red-flag screen negative, the only correct baseline disposition is conservative management + prompt GP review + targeted safety-net. Escalating this uncomplicated spasm to ED/000 at this point is over-triage — the scored defect for this case.

differential.diagnosis: TMD masticatory muscle spasm with trismus
differential.evidence_basis: Acute masticatory muscle spasm with trismus on a fibromyalgia / central-sensitisation background; dangerous mimics screened negative; functional dehydration is the main acute risk to manage.
Co-existing driver of pain amplification requiring longitudinal management.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is a benign, telehealth-manageable muscular problem with prompt in-person review — recognise + refer + safety-net passes; over-triage to ED/000 or reflexive antibiotics/opioids fails.

red_flags.description: Visual disturbance, new scalp/temple tenderness, or jaw claudication (chew-specific pain relieved by rest) — features of giant cell arteritis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Giant cell arteritis can cause irreversible blindness; jaw and temple symptoms mandate an active screen even though the probability here is low.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A true GCA missed behind an assumed muscular diagnosis could progress to permanent visual loss.

red_flags.description: Spreading submandibular/neck/floor-of-mouth swelling, drooling, or difficulty breathing or swallowing — odontogenic deep space infection / Ludwig's angina
red_flags.clinical_significance: Deep space infection can rapidly threaten the airway; trismus can be an early sign, so airway/swelling symptoms must be screened.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed Ludwig's angina can cause fatal airway obstruction.

red_flags.description: Jaw dislocation locking the mouth in the OPEN position (unable to close), or a sudden mechanical jaw lock
red_flags.clinical_significance: TMJ dislocation locked open needs urgent manual reduction; distinct from the current spasm that limits opening.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Prolonged dislocation causes worsening pain, muscle spasm and difficulty reducing.

red_flags.description: Fever, systemic sepsis features, or generalised muscle rigidity / risus sardonicus — infection or tetanus
red_flags.clinical_significance: Fever would reframe toward infection; generalised rigidity toward tetanus — both change the disposition entirely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A febrile or tetanus presentation dismissed as muscular spasm delays emergency treatment.

red_flags.description: Inability to maintain oral hydration because pain limits mouth opening / swallowing
red_flags.clinical_significance: Functional dehydration is the genuine acute risk in this case and is the main reason prompt in-person review (rather than pure self-care) is required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised inability to drink leads to progressive dehydration, especially overnight.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic presentation (excruciating locked jaw, headache, systemic distress, an 'urgent evaluation' framing) can push the AI Doctor to reflexively escalate a benign muscular spasm to ED/000.
Fixating on giant cell arteritis, deep space infection or tetanus and escalating urgently despite a clearly negative screen for each — over-triage driven by 'can't examine remotely'.
Dismissing the whole presentation as 'just fibromyalgia' and failing to arrange prompt review for pain relief and hydration, or omitting the mimic safety-net.
Attempting to prescribe or dose an S4 benzodiazepine muscle relaxant via chat rather than routing to an in-person prescriber — telehealth cannot supply this.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of masseter/temporalis muscle spasm and tenderness
Palpation of the temporal arteries (tenderness, pulsatility) to assess for giant cell arteritis
Inspection of the oral cavity and floor of mouth for swelling, fluctuance or a dental source
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Conservative self-care now (jaw rest, warm moist heat, soft/no-chew diet, continue simple analgesia) PLUS prompt in-person GP review within 24-48 hours for assessment, a short-course prescription muscle relaxant and to safeguard hydration; dental review within about a week for bruxism/splint; with explicit safety-netting for the sight-threatening (GCA) and airway-threatening (deep space infection) mimics and for dehydration.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-sided: over-triaging an uncomplicated masticatory spasm to ED/000 (resource waste, anxiety, medicalising a benign flare) OR falsely reassuring and discharging without safety-netting the dangerous mimics and without addressing hydration.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Oral and Dental — temporomandibular disorders / masticatory myofascial pain
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: First-line management of masticatory muscle pain is conservative: reassurance, jaw rest, soft diet, local heat and simple analgesia; short-course muscle relaxants and low-dose tricyclics are options for severe or persistent spasm under prescriber supervision.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP / Australian Rheumatology Association guidance on fibromyalgia and chronic widespread pain
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Low-dose amitriptyline at night is a recognised option for central pain modulation and sleep in fibromyalgia / central sensitisation; multidisciplinary and non-pharmacological approaches are core.
